title: Episode 16 symbolism Leliel and Shinji
wikitext:
[]: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Mon Jan 18, 2021 11:52 am Hello you all. I'd like to debate with you one of the episodes I like the most. There are two aspects that I want to discuss: 1. Sunset scenario and train. I really like when Anno put this kind scenario in the philosophy/psycholgy scenes and dialogues. I do not actually know what is the puropose but probably it can be these: Sunset because Japan is the country of sunrise and the first state in the world that sees the sun rising each day (I guess this could be an important fact to consider why in EVA and other anime tend to put this scenarios refelcting the sun going down and the day "dying" when something important occurs). Also probably the train is an icon of "trip" where Shinji goes somewhere (even you can hear an average railroad crossing siren...) close to him or is just a memory when he was a kid abandoned by Gendo. 2. The dialogues between Shinji and leliel. Probably you remember this scene but I want to point out this one: Leliel: "you do not know how to swim right?" Shinji: "Humans were not born to swim" What do you think this could mean? I guess in my humble opinion this is a self-belief of Shinji about not doing something that are natural impossible. I mean if we literally did not born knowing how to swim it is normal not knowing to. But in this case this is why Leliel criticizes him of giving up or not trying something new. Well these were my thoughts. Thanks if someone reads it!

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Derantor » Mon Jan 18, 2021 12:37 pm Hey there! I remember a recent private discussion about this topic which mostly resulted in the conclusion that more work needs to be done to understand the sunset symbolism exactly. As food for thought, here are some things to consider: - Sunset is generally a transitionary period. Day turns into night - something light turns into something dark. Assosciations are generally negative. Of course, a sunrise has the opposite symbolism. I think it's telling that we can't clearly tell whether or not the sun is rising or sinking. Shinji is undergoing a mental transition - but the destination is not clear. - Day and night are represented by the sun and moon. Rei is assosciated with the moon, Asuka with the sun. Shinji with sunset, in this case. Maybe a connection along these lines can be found too. - The train indeed seems to be connected to Shinji's traumatic experience of being abandoned with his father. Those sequences are often called "Hell Train Sequences" - we are viewing Shinji's personal (inner) hell. As for Leliel: That line is indeed pretty cryptic, since it really does not connect logically to what came before. Your guess is as good as mine here. That Shinji is afraid of change is something that NTE (aka Rebuild) spells out explicitely. But since that is a different continuity, we have to look if NGE handles him the same way; and in my opinion, it does. He never gave up on Cello because nobody told him to - he preferred the status quo over change, even positive change, in case he did not want to play the cello any longer. He also exclaims "I have found this thing I like - why can't I just do that forever?" (quoting from memory here, so, inaccurately - in right now not even sure if he said that in NTE or NGE). In EoE, he builds a pyramid, destroys it, then rebuilds it again. That too points to him not wanting change; he doesn't do new things. So, the lines he shares with Leliel pointing to a similar unwillingness to change or try out new things seems entirely reasonable to me.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Mon Jan 18, 2021 1:11 pm SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Zusuchan » Mon Jan 18, 2021 1:19 pm Please do not quote entire posts, but only the parts most relevant to what you yourself will say. Quoting is not also always necessary. I put your quote into a spoiler tab to make your post into lighter reading, but I would like it if you were to heed by what I said. Thank you. Also hi and welcome to the forum!

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Mon Jan 18, 2021 1:56 pm So sorry I do not know yet how to quote properly. The next time it will be better Thanks for the advisement!

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Mon Jan 18, 2021 2:30 pm That line is indeed pretty cryptic, since it really does not connect logically to what came before. Your guess is as good as mine here. That Shinji is afraid of change is something that NTE (aka Rebuild) spells out explicitely. But since that is a different continuity, we have to look if NGE handles him the same way; and in my opinion, it does.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Tue Jan 19, 2021 12:21 am What you have pointed out about piloting the EVA is so interesting. I remember in NGE when Gendo for the first in his life congrats Shinji for piloting the EVA and fighting against the angels. Since then Shinji starts looking like more happy but as Leliel said in this episode that's a "fake happiness" because he doesn't really want to be an EVA pilot and doesn't know how to give up on the status quo.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Jan 19, 2021 3:05 am Javi2541997 wrote: 1. Sunset scenario and train. I really like when Anno put this kind scenario in the philosophy/psycholgy scenes and dialogues. I do not actually know what is the puropose but probably it can be these: Sunset because Japan is the country of sunrise and the first state in the world that sees the sun rising each day

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Derantor » Tue Jan 19, 2021 8:04 am Javi2541997 wrote: that's a "fake happiness" because he doesn't really want to be an EVA pilot and doesn't know how to give up on the status quo.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Tue Jan 19, 2021 8:19 am UrsusArctos wrote: Another reason for Japan's association with the sun is because of the central role of the sun goddess Amaterasu O-Mikami in the Japanese Shinto religion. Whether the mythology surrounding Amaterasu has anything to do with the sun being behind Rei in the train scenes is an open question. I'm sure I saw some old discussion about that but I'd have to look it up later.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Zusuchan » Tue Jan 19, 2021 8:36 am Do not double post unless you either consider it necessary or a significant amount of time has passed from your previous post. Thank you. Whether or not Kaworu really is altruistic as you say can be a bit up to question depending on one's viewpoint, heh. Some great ideas here . I'm sure there's been other discussions about that previously on the forum somewhere, but as a rather new user, I'm afraid I don't have a good idea of what they would be. Anyway, I just feel like that's a great little thread that deserves to be read more, and the ideas presented there don't really differ from your viewpoint of Kaworu as "altruistic" either, though they do theorize that it maybe wasn't only humankind Kaworu allowed himself to be killed for...

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Jan 19, 2021 9:14 am 1. It's probably a composite of memories - It's implied that baby Shinji got sent on a train by himself to his teacher's place. (in Japan it's usual for small kids to go to places on their own but for such a big change you'd probably want emotional support) The common flashback shot features him at what appears to be a train station, the shadowy image of Gendo walking off into the distance, telling him not to run off again (which he presumably did following Yui's death, as alluded in eps 20 and 16)... The shirt he's wearing barely fits, and he's a bit older than during the experiment (said to be 4 rather than 3); Gendo must have been rather overwhelmed with dressing & minding him etc. There's a separate incident in 2010 - it's implied that Shinji confronted Gendo about Yui's death & his general absence, and ended also running off - We're told that this is the last time they saw each other. (as per ep 1) though this also implies that Gendo used to visit at least sporadically before that. It's also possible that he saw Rei I, as she appears in one of the flashbacks in the same lighting. Maybe Gendo had her with him; I imagine that would've made 9 year old Shinji really salty/jelly. He might not have made the connection before ep 16 or 20 because a) he was young & seems to have rather repressed the incident and b) if Rei I had continued growing normally she's be 9 in 2015, so you wouldn't think it's the same person. For being the MC, there are actually quite a few 'mysterious' bits in Shinji's past that are sufficiently implied but never exactly shown When we see kid!Shinji in the train scenes, he's wearing the same outfit, holding the same bag. It's possible that on the day that he got sent away there really was a bright sunset, but it also makes for a gloomy mood and dramatic lighting, allowing the characters to be partially obscured. Sunset is a liminal time just like a train/ train station is a liminal space. It's not a place in itself but a place between places, or that's how our minds tend to parse it which is why such places can appear creepy or especially aesthetic, not quite real... The last "train scene" in EoE of course cummulates in Shinji talking about how he is afraid of ambiguity and uncertainty, which a liminal place & time could symbolize. Of course Shinji also rides plenty of trains in his time in Tokyo-3 - when he ran off in ep 4 for example. He also does physically, literally ride the same train as Rei & presumably talk with her frequently (in ep 5 we see them on the same train to HQ, in ep 22 we see them talking at the station) so of course that would reoccur in his dreams/visions. In any case, through such painful memories as when he was sent off as a kid and when he ran off in ep 4, the train setting probably came to symbolize a state of anxious distress for him. In a sense he is "stuck" in that moment when he's 4 and alone, living his life dominated by the fear of that same rejection. Like in the first episode of DS9 where the wormhole aliens tell Sisko that he "exists here" about the moment his first wife died. 2. There's a general theme of Shinji seeing the world as fixed and himself as having no choice & no escape. This is dissasembled/discussed at length in the last two eps later. It's true that people don't float, but there ARE humans who learned to swim, so saying that the reason you can't swim is that humans don't naturally float is an excuse. The truth is probably that he's afraid of the water, or, never mustered the time & energy to try & learn it. His parents weren't around to teach him... etc. but he doesn't want to confront that. Note that Leliel is reading his mind at this point & hence pointing out things that Shinji is keeping outside his awareness at this point. Leliel probably thinks it curious that humans do that. Just like the humans are investigating the angels.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Tue Jan 19, 2021 10:34 am He's not acting because of concern for the greater good, he wants to get something out of it. That is not to say that Shinji is a bad person, not at all. He is concerned about others, and he clearly shows it throughout the series

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Tue Jan 19, 2021 10:59 am Kendrix wrote: Note that Leliel is reading his mind at this point & hence pointing out things that Shinji is keeping outside his awareness at this point. Leliel probably thinks it curious that humans do that. Just like the humans are investigating the angels.

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Jan 20, 2021 5:37 am ^A really good decision to set EVA apart from the crowd. Hm, I'm not sure it was an attack, though it's certainly capturing a specimen in an environment where it can only survive so long & then poking around in it When you think about it it's a reverse of the Sandalphon situation (humans wanting to catch & study an angel but it escaped them) Indeed, it's kinda ambiguous if even Arael knew what it was doing. - Was it an attack (like Misato & ritsuko theorize) that requires knowing enough about human thinking to know what would constitute mental torture? Is it trying to fry her brain as an organ but doesn't know or care what overstimulating her nerves causes her to experience? Or is it just trying to investigate, possibly having no idea that it's frying/torturing her? And Armisael was clearly after unit zero itself to do something ... maybe revive its brethren. Absorbing Rei was only collateral (though it seems surprised that she wouldn't want to be absorbed)

[]: Re: Episode 16 symbolism: Leliel and Shinji.             Quote Post by Javi2541997 » Wed Jan 20, 2021 7:56 am Kendrix wrote: Or is it just trying to investigate, possibly having no idea that it's frying/torturing her?
